speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is April the 16th, 1996. Survivor's name is Shmuel Huber. The interviewer is Vera Goldberg in Sydney, New South Wales, Australia. And the language is in English. This is an interview with Shmuel Huber. My name is Vera Goldberg. We are in Sydney, Australia. And the language of the interview is English. Can you tell me your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: First name or--

speaker: interviewer
text: Both.

speaker: interviewee
text: Both-- Sam Huber.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you-- are you always called Sam?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. At home, I was called Shmuel.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell Shmuel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shmuel-- in Hebrew or in English?

speaker: interviewer
text: English.

speaker: interviewee
text: English-- S-H-M-U-E-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your surname?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huber-- H-U-B-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Shmuel, do you have any Hebrew name? What's your Hebrew name?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the Hebrew name, [INAUDIBLE] Shmuel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes? And in shul, what are you called?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same.

speaker: interviewer
text: Same.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Always Shmuel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did you have any nicknames, any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never. Never.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell me, when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: 7th of April, 1923.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how old are you now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Shmuel, where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Falenica. This is a suburb of Warsaw-- more or less a suburb of Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far from Warsaw downtown would Falenica be?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train, or-- we went, usually by train. It would be about 20 minutes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was a suburban part of the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yes, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- were you born in Falenica?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you grew up there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till-- yes, till I left when the Germans invaded.

speaker: interviewer
text: So up until the arrival of the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, till I left, September, '39, or October, '39. That would be more correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: What can I say? My father had a shop of books.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, what type of books were they, Shmuel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Both secular and religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the shop was in Falenica?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live close to the shop?

speaker: interviewee
text: The shop, the house, the home, the kitchen-- very, very congested-- very small place.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were actually-- your father lived and worked--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the premises.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, on the premises.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you a big family? How many people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had, I had an older brother-- elder brother and two sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Abraham Aaron.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Golda Leah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you in the family? What child were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The youngest.

speaker: interviewer
text: The baby.

speaker: interviewee
text: The baby at that time, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me about your, your brother and sisters. Who was the oldest?

speaker: interviewee
text: The oldest was Hannah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how much older than you would she have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't remember Hannah. The second sister was Miriam, and she was five years older than I am-- than I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and how about your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was two and 1/2 years older.

speaker: interviewer
text: So two girls, and then two boys?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about your parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: You'll have to be more precise in questioning me. What shall I tell you about? We had a very hard life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, what, what sort of man was your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Strictly religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: How strictly religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stricter than strict-- that should tell you anything, a fundamentalist.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he belong to a particular sect of Judaism?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know sect. Sect doesn't fit him, the word. A Hasidic Jew, he was. But I don't reckon this is a sect.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were a Hasidic family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you follow any particular rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ger, or Ger, you could pronounce it, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Rebbe of Gur.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This was a quite a well-known in Poland among the Hasidic Jews. He was numero uno, I would say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the Ger Hasidim a large group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very-- yes, comparatively large, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father was a Ger Hasid?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: His father as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't know much about that. I don't remember this, so many years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- so your father, besides, his book business--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was very involved with, with Torah and study.

speaker: interviewee
text: The only-- the only interest, this, yes-- work for a livelihood and study, pray and study.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that, that really was his--

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What his life was all about.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of man was he in terms of personality?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm very hesitant to talk about it, because I would be partial, obviously. But I did not get along with him very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was not an easygoing man?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he very strict?

speaker: interviewee
text: Extremely strict.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- an-- what-- an authoritarian type of father.

speaker: interviewee
text: Affirmative.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very affirmative.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very affirmative to your question.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: The opposite.

speaker: interviewer
text: The opposite.

speaker: interviewee
text: The opposite-- very pleasant, very soft, very accommodating. I do not remember my mother having an exchange-- I mean, being strict or having to raise her voice ever in her life-- with us, with neighbors, with anybody-- never, till the last day when I saw her, so just opposite.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- so was that because you were particularly obedient children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had no choice in that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you followed the rules.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were rigid rules.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very rigid.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were followed perfectly.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were perfectly. I don't like to say this-- doesn't fit in the word.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were rigidly adhered to.

speaker: interviewee
text: Rigidly adhered-- that would be the correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Was your mother as religious as your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She was religious, but more progressive, I would say would be the word.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was her type of religion different from your father's?

speaker: interviewee
text: If I would have to fit in one word, I will say more enlightened.

speaker: interviewer
text: More enlightened.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she-- did, did your family have secular newspapers and secular books at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Secular books, yes, secular newspapers, not in my memory-- you have to understand that father was very controlling. And it would be very hard to bring in newspaper, but books, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father loved books?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my father did not love books. My, my sisters loved books. And she, as a matter of fact, she-- my younger sister in particularly-- introduced me to reading, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What books did she introduce you to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, she was leftist in her outlook, very leftist in her outlook. Yes, she was a communist sympathizer, I would dare say. As it is so many years ago, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I wouldn't be sure if that is so. I know that she was leftist, very leftist in her outlook. But you have to realize that Jewish people in Poland were so oppressed that to be leftist was very easy, you know, just to look for something that-- for redemption, so to say. So she was politically minded, and I would say leftist, at least.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was your second sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Both sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Both sisters.

speaker: interviewee
text: Both were, yes. Both were like this, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your parents? Did they have these kind of views as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, none whatsoever. No, of course not.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was a great divergence from the parental thinking.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father sold secular books in his shop?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes-- not secular books, but books for school, you know? In Poland, you didn't get, like here in school, you got all the books. In the shop, you sold the books that were required for school opening during the year. So these were the books. I don't know if you can call it secular-- for school, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Textbooks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Textbooks, that is correct, and writing materials as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the actual books that he sold were Jewish books.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, both. Other--

speaker: interviewer
text: Other than the textbooks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Other than that, other than that were Hebrew books.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, do you remember your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They-- when I was born, I don't think they were alive anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father and mother have brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So do you remember having uncles and aunts and cousins?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they live in Falenica as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, none of them, none of them. From my mother's side, some were living in Warsaw proper. And some were living in a city called Opoczno in Poland. That would be about two hours, I would say, travel by train-- two to three hours travel by train from Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever get together with your extended family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not frequently, only on occasions. In Warsaw, where I learned, where I studied from the age of 12 till 14, I was a boarder in the school. So I used to go to my mother's sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because she lived in Warsaw, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, a Saturday afternoon, you know, for a visit-- or i used to go to my father's brother, who lived in the center of Warsaw as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the other family who lived--

speaker: interviewee
text: Other family lived further away with no money to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the contact was very--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, wasn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Very limited.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very limited.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about your education.

speaker: interviewee
text: Unfortunate, not secular whatsoever.

speaker: interviewer
text: You never had a secular education?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, none whatsoever. What I know is just self-acquired.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you start going to a Jewish-type school?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the age of three.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the school that you went to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cheder.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went to cheder at the age of three.

speaker: interviewee
text: At the age of three, yes-- sound, sounds strange, no?

speaker: interviewer
text: It's hard for a three-year-old.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What hours did you go to cheder?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the morning till late in the night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even at the age of three.

speaker: interviewee
text: This I wouldn't say. This I don't remember, but later, at the age of seven, eight, definitely. We used to come home-- I used to come home 3 o'clock or 2:30 to, to have-- I don't know what you call it, would be lunch or dinner-- and go back to study and come back late in the night-- 9 o'clock, after 8. This was the day's study.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you learn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very hard-- what should I tell you what I learned? Subjects, we had-- the subjects-- there's no such thing in, in Jewish schooling as finishing school. You know, you can learn all your life and know just a little bit. I learned religious studies. That's all. It's very hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you learned to read and write Hebrew.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no read and write Hebrew didn't-- doesn't come into this. No, it's an entirely different field. As a matter of fact, just the opposite-- in, in religious, strict religious studies, you just ignore modern Hebrew or modern language, Hebrew language, as that was taboo, just religious studies, which was mostly Aramaic-- old Hebrew and Aramaic.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this started at--

speaker: interviewee
text: Talmud, Talmud, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, so Torah, Talmud.

speaker: interviewee
text: Torah, Talmud, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: This started at the age of three and continued on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Age of three and continued progressively advanced, more and more, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: The cheder was for boys only?

speaker: interviewee
text: No coeducational schools in our home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who taught you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember any names. To begin, the cheder was in a private home-- very poor. There was no electricity, no water, no timber floor. It had a kerosene lamp-- absolutely primitive. And I try to remember now, it's like, I would say, in Third World countries now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the cold? Do you remember the heat and the cold?

speaker: interviewee
text: Heat, I don't remember so much. But cold, definitely, because heating was a problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you sit at desks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes-- not a desk. I don't know what it was. Some kind of improvised.

speaker: interviewer
text: Table or chair.

speaker: interviewee
text: Table, chair, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children would have been in the cheder?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say in the Cheder would be 12 to 15.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you enjoy going to cheder?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not to my knowledge, [LAUGHTER] no, no, I wouldn't say so. It was obligatory, and that's it. You accept. I have learned from the very beginning to accept things that cannot be resisted.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the way it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the way it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you didn't-- one didn't change those things.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had no choice in that. Democracy didn't exist. The word democracy, I learned much later in my life-- choice, democracy, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you wear? What was the garments of a Ger Hasid?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the garments, that was-- how can I describe it? Dark, drab-- it was a ghetto clothing, you know, that it was imposed on Jews hundreds of years ago. So later, they accepted it on their own volition.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you looked conspicuously different from every else who wasn't Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: From-- that is, that is correct. That is correct. Or [? wasn't ?] religious-- there were some Jews who didn't wear that, that wore modern clothes. There were, of course, quite a number.

speaker: interviewer
text: But if you were a Hasid, then--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then it was obligatory. You couldn't have any other way.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had payot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes-- sidelocks, you mean.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sidelocks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely, yes. That was a problem, by the way. Walking in Warsaw with sidelocks was courting danger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I would say in Warsaw, while I was studying in yeshiva, whenever I went out in the streets, I was kicked or abused-- verbally abused, assaulted.

speaker: interviewer
text: On a daily basis?

speaker: interviewee
text: More or less-- the exception would be to be in a tolerant surrounding.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were bashed up every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, kicked. It would be just-- not bashed up is a bit more, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Pushed, kicked.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pushed, kicked, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all, all Hasidim who were going about their business would have experienced similar treatment?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would, I would say so. I would say that Aborigines here have got a very good life in comparison with our life, yes. And besides, in Warsaw there was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the Jewish part, the part the Jews lived, our life was a ghetto life. You were much safer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Within the Jewish areas?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but once you went out further, you know, when I, when I went to my parents' home and I had to go to the train, then when danger starts.

speaker: interviewer
text: So if you were traveling backwards and forwards on trains--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- yes, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was any protection ever given by the police or anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, no such thing. You are talking about things that didn't exist.

speaker: interviewer
text: So nobody--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody. This-- oh, yeah, that how it was. This is how life was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back a little bit to Falenica. When you walked to cheder every day, was that a similar kind of situation?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember I was always living in fear, yes. I remember I always fearful, looking back here, there. What is happening? Who is coming?

speaker: interviewer
text: As a little boy, as a three-year-old and a five-year-old?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. I don't know three. I don't remember three. I-- my memory begins, I would say-- it's starting to crystallize at the age of five.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you think back to fiive--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is fear part of those--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, most definitely.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were always frightened.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any positive encounters with non-Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember, interesting thing, one-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] no, I would, I would go back. We had neighbors who were more tolerant. We were neighbors-- we had neighbors who were not so bad. But particular, I remember one who was a schoolteacher. He used to come to buy books for the school. And he-- I always look at him like some kind of, like a Polish angel. Because he was very gentle, very pleasant and polite. This is the exception, I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was the one person--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was outstanding, because he was different.

speaker: interviewer
text: He treated you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Very nice, oh, he was very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Politely and nicely.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I was-- again, so we had neighbors who were more tolerant. They knew us, so-- attacked-- you were always attacked by a stranger, not by a neighbor. Neighbors had a certain tolerance.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the neighborhood that you lived in Falenica a very mixed neighborhood, or predominantly one or the other?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say it was not an enforced segregation. But it was-- different parts of the city were Jewish and different parts were Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: But within your street, there were Polish people and Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, mixed-- yeah, yeah, yeah, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your neighbors would have been both?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever visit a non-Jewish home? Did you ever go into a Christian house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the, the two peoples really kept--

speaker: interviewee
text: Separate lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: Totally apart.

speaker: interviewee
text: Different language, different culture.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you speak Polish? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Were you able to? Did you understand Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I understand Polish. Even now, I understand Polish. But I spoke Polish very hesitantly.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was because you just never used the language?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never learned it. It was never my language.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you poor?

speaker: interviewee
text: It depends what you say. In, in today's terms, I would say definitely, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in relative terms of those times, in that place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very hard living, very hard living-- we could never afford what we wanted, never. Even in food, we were limited to what we could afford and what we couldn't afford.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- tell me a little bit about the, the food that you had. Did you have enough to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Enough, yes, but a very restricted diet-- bread, potatoes, not much meat, cake once a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: When, when was cake served?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cake-- Shabbat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Only Shabbat.

speaker: interviewee
text: Only Shabbat. Got a slice, and that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about meat?

speaker: interviewee
text: The cheapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have it on days other than Shabbat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a bit of meat--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: The rest of the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But ask me about butter or coffee or tea-- here came in the restrictions. Tea, you used to rinse the same-- we used to buy tea in 10-gram bags. You used to rinse and rinse, since the color was colorless.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you used--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Every, every drop of tea flavor.

speaker: interviewee
text: Every drop, and more than that. And coffee, used-- we couldn't afford coffee. So we bought a product called chicory. I don't-- [INAUDIBLE] I think you have it here in, in Australia-- that gives color, but tastes different. Just people should think we have coffee.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about holidays? Where there ever any times when your father wasn't working?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, every Sunday was observed holiday, because it was state-enforced, every Saturday because we were Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your father, in effect, had a weekend.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Saturday was strict. Sunday was official. And unofficially, you know, then you observed it much less.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about holidays from school or from cheder and then later yeshiva?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] 16 days a year-- eight days Pesach, Passover, and eight days in Sukkot.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were the--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two times a year when there was no learning.

speaker: interviewee
text: No learning.

speaker: interviewer
text: How often did you go to the [INAUDIBLE] or synagogue, shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: While I was young, I would say at least twice a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Morning and evening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Morning and evening, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And of course, on the holidays, the big festivals, all the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Spend half a day, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Except for all day on Yom Kippur.

speaker: interviewee
text: All day Yom Kippur and most of the day on Rosh Hashanah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So other than the two times a year of Pesach and Sukkot when there was a holiday--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was other holidays, if you know. There's Shavuot.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they were the only times-- the Jewish holidays--

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the only times--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the routine of cheder changed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was interrupted, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about, what about your, you father? Did he-- do you think he was happy with his life and his lot?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do not imagine, because life was very hard. Financially, it was-- we were very-- how should I say? Insecure, can I say so? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] To meet a payment was always a problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you were aware of the difficulties?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, of course I was aware, of course. We couldn't afford anything. An item of new clothes was always an event.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember getting new clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not particularly, no. My mother had a brother who lived in Opoczno, as I said. And he was very well-off. And he used, from time to time, Opoczno to help us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So occasionally your uncle would--

speaker: interviewee
text: Occasionally, yes, would send money.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then there would be something.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not every-- that's not every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you remain in cheder? Until what age?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till the age of 12.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened when you were 12?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was sent to Warsaw to-- for exam.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of exam?

speaker: interviewee
text: Different to what you see today-- the exam was that the principal called you in. And he said, well, what do you know about this item or that item, he asked. And if he liked, if you answered very satisfactory, it means [INAUDIBLE]. And I was at the 12-year-- I was then 12 years old. That was in 1935. And I won't-- it sounds a bit boasting, but was a very gifted.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they decided you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: They decided I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: They wanted you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and I went to yeshiva in Warsaw then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the yeshiva have a particular name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, mesivta.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mesivta yeshiva.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, mesivta, just mesivta.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just mesivta.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mesivta is Aramiac for yeshiva.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was a very prestigious place of learning?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in Warsaw, yes, very much so-- even in Poland, not just in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a famous school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many boys do you think would have been there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say in the, in the hundreds.

speaker: interviewer
text: Several hundred boys.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you lived at the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This was, by the way, located in a place where the Warsaw Uprising, the last, in 1943, was in this street.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was what-- in what was to become the center of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto uprising-- the ghetto was much larger, because there were half a million Jews at that time in the ghetto. But this was the last stand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Exactly at the place of the mesivta.

speaker: interviewee
text: At the place-- not-- the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: In that street.

speaker: interviewee
text: That place was-- the Germans employed there-- I think brush, they manufactured brushes there, I think. I'm not sure. Anyway, this was the place where the Jewish proletariat, can I say so? [? Prolets, ?] proletariat-- the left wing of the ALP, in modern terms, existed there. And there was--

speaker: interviewer
text: That was where the mesivta was.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's stop there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
